incidents.victim: US federal civilian agency (unnamed) and other Cisco ASA/Firepower operators
incidents.vector: Exploited Cisco ASA WebVPN zero-days (CVE-2025-20333, CVE-2025-20362) to drop the FIRESTARTER backdoor and Line Viper implant, which survive firmware patches and standard reboots by manipulating the Cisco Service Platform mount list.
incidents.impact: Attackers regained access to a compromised Firepower device in March 2026 — nearly six months after initial breach — and harvested admin credentials, certificates, and private keys; CISA updated Emergency Directive 25-03, ordering federal agencies to snapshot Cisco device memory and tying the activity to the ArcaneDoor UAT-4356 cluster aligned with Chinese state interests.

incidents.victim: Global organizations targeted by Chinese state hackers; compromised SOHO routers/IoT owners
incidents.vector: State-backed groups are routing attacks through industrial-scale covert botnets — largely compromised small-office/home-office routers, IoT devices, and edge gear maintained by Chinese infosec contractors (e.g., the Raptor Train network of 200,000+ devices run by Integrity Technology Group).
incidents.impact: Thirteen Five Eyes and allied agencies issued a joint advisory warning that Volt Typhoon-style pre-positioning and Flax Typhoon espionage now rely on these rotating networks, causing 'IOC extinction' that defeats static IP blocklists and gives hackers low-cost, deniable reach into US and allied critical infrastructure.

incidents.vector: Attackers traced to Context.ai, a third-party AI tool used by a Vercel employee whose laptop was infected with Lumma Stealer malware (delivered via a Roblox cheats lure in February); stolen OAuth tokens and access keys enabled enumeration of Vercel environment variables across customer accounts.
incidents.impact: After analyzing nearly a petabyte of logs, Vercel confirmed more customers than originally disclosed were impacted; ShinyHunters claims responsibility and is selling access keys, source code, and database extracts — exposing customers that use Next.js (9 million weekly downloads) to downstream compromise.

incidents.victim: Mongolian government institution (12 backdoored systems) and likely dozens more
incidents.vector: ESET-tracked 'GopherWhisper' cluster deployed a rotating set of custom backdoors (LaxGopher, CompactGopher, RatGopher, BoxOfFriends, SSLORDoor) that abuse Slack, Discord, Microsoft Outlook draft folders, and file.io for command-and-control.
incidents.impact: Operator has maintained espionage access against Mongolian government targets since at least late 2023, exfiltrating documents via public cloud services; operator tooling and internal notes ('How to write RATs') suggest a less sophisticated but highly persistent China-aligned team filling Mongolia's thin cyber-defense landscape.

incidents.vector: Operators used LinkedIn-based fake job offers from AI-generated shell companies (including a Mexico-registered front) to trick developers into downloading malicious coding-assessment tools that deploy BeaverTail, OtterCookie, and InvisibleFerret info-stealers.
incidents.impact: Expel researchers estimate North Korea-linked 'Famous Chollima'/'HexagonalRodent' stole up to $12M in cryptocurrency in Q1 2026 from individual developers; 31 operators across six teams ran the campaign and are pivoting resources from the fading DPRK IT-worker fraud scheme.

incidents.victim: Enterprise victims globally (100+ per quarter, incl. government, healthcare, manufacturing)
incidents.vector: The Gentlemen RaaS affiliates gain initial access through internet-facing devices, deploy SystemBC proxy malware and Cobalt Strike for lateral movement, then abuse Active Directory Group Policy to detonate Go-based ransomware across every domain-joined host simultaneously (plus an ESXi variant that evades antivirus).
incidents.impact: Since appearing in mid-2025, The Gentlemen claimed 202 victims last quarter (second only to Qilin) and was tied to a 1,570-node SystemBC botnet; 90% affiliate payouts are attracting operators and make it a frontrunner to replace more mature RaaS brands.

incidents.victim: 200+ organizations and 5,000 consumer devices across 18,000+ compromised routers
incidents.vector: GRU operators (tracked by Microsoft as Forest Blizzard) chained known flaws in end-of-life Mikrotik and TP-Link SOHO routers to hijack DNS settings and point victims to attacker-controlled resolvers, silently intercepting Microsoft Office OAuth authentication tokens without deploying any malware on endpoints.
incidents.impact: At its December 2025 peak, the campaign harvested tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks — primarily ministries of foreign affairs, law enforcement agencies, and third-party email providers — enabling persistent mailbox access; Microsoft and Black Lotus Labs published details and NCSC-UK issued a parallel advisory.

incidents.victim: Israeli water treatment and desalination utilities (targeted but not successfully compromised)
incidents.vector: 'ZionSiphon' OT malware (attributed by Darktrace with high likelihood to Iranian actors) checks for Israeli IP ranges and hardcoded Israeli water-utility strings before pushing preset configurations to raise pump pressure and chlorine levels to maximum.
incidents.impact: A XOR encoding bug in the IP-range check caused the malware to self-delete in every observed case — but political strings ('Poisoning the population of Tel Aviv and Haifa') and the OT-specific logic confirm intent to sabotage Israel's water supply; Darktrace could not confirm whether it was deployed in the wild.

incidents.victim: 170+ Ukrainian prosecutors and military targets in Greece, Romania, and Serbia
incidents.vector: GRU operators (tracked as APT28) compromised email accounts to siphon investigative and defense correspondence; campaign detected by Ctrl-Alt-Intel and reported by Reuters.
incidents.impact: Attackers accessed Ukrainian prosecutorial investigations and defense-ministry email at NATO partners in southeast Europe, extending Moscow's intelligence reach into war-related legal proceedings and allied military planning.

incidents.vector: Unspecified intrusion forced the city to shut down its IT network; surrounding Leon County preemptively disconnected its network to prevent 'contamination' — language typical of ransomware containment.
incidents.impact: Multiple city websites and public services went offline and remained unavailable for days; Leon County's defensive disconnection suggests responders anticipated lateral spread consistent with a ransomware event.

incidents.vector: Attackers drained hot wallets belonging to the OFAC-sanctioned exchange; Grinex publicly blamed 'Western intelligence agencies' without providing evidence.
incidents.impact: Approximately $13M (≈1 billion rubles) stolen; exchange shuttered operations — a blow to a platform US authorities designated last August for laundering ransomware proceeds and helping Russia evade sanctions.

incidents.vector: Sophisticated distributed denial-of-service attack peaked overnight on April 15-16 and intensified throughout the following day; follow-on waves continued through the week.
incidents.impact: Platform operated intermittently for nearly a day and required sustained mitigation work; comes days after a similar DDoS campaign disrupted Mastodon's flagship server, suggesting coordinated targeting of decentralized social platforms.

incidents.victim: LiteLLM users, Checkmarx GitHub Actions customers, downstream consumers
incidents.vector: Group calling itself 'TeamPCP' used stolen maintainer credentials to push credential-harvesting payloads into LiteLLM (97M monthly downloads) and to poison two Checkmarx GitHub Actions workflows; an earlier compromise of Aqua Security's Trivy infrastructure provided the initial foothold across five ecosystems in five days.
incidents.impact: Malware silently harvested AWS keys, cloud credentials, and AI API keys from every installing organization and exfiltrated them to a lookalike domain; group publicly claims 300+ GB of stolen data and ransomware-affiliate monetization, with campaign still expanding at time of reporting.

incidents.vector: Attackers exploited a vulnerability in the Kelp smart-contract platform to withdraw funds as rsETH (restaked Ether) tokens.
incidents.impact: Approximately $292M drained — now the largest crypto heist of 2026; subsequent reporting ties the attack and a similar theft from Drift to North Korean state-backed operators, continuing Pyongyang's high-tempo targeting of DeFi platforms.

incidents.vector: Attackers seeded fake token pools on the protocol over several days, then exploited weaknesses in the margin-trading feature to route transactions through attacker-controlled pools.
incidents.impact: About $18.4M in assets drained; premeditated setup demonstrates the advance reconnaissance and infrastructure staging now typical in DeFi protocol attacks.

incidents.vector: ShinyHunters extracted customer records from Hallmark's Salesforce instance during a 2025 wave of OAuth-token-based attacks on Salesforce customers.
incidents.impact: Personal data for 1.7 million customers leaked publicly after Hallmark refused to pay a ransom; consistent with ShinyHunters' broader 2025-2026 pattern of monetizing compromised SaaS data via extortion and dark-web sales.

incidents.vector: Crimson Collective obtained a GitHub Personal Access Token inadvertently published on a public-facing website, then used TruffleHog to scan thousands of repositories for additional secrets, pivoted to Azure cloud storage via Microsoft Graph API, and attempted to inject malicious code into multiple repos for future secret harvesting.
incidents.impact: First documented Talos IR engagement with Crimson Collective; data exfiltrated from Azure storage and attempted persistent implants in the victim's repositories illustrate the growing trend of criminal actors living off legitimate developer tools and cloud APIs to evade detection.
